_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Surry County, VA. Record survival for the south-bank James River county — records survive from the 1650s, with relatively minor Civil War losses.

current_courthouse.location: Surry, VA — Route 10 at the courthouse village

previous_courthouses.location: Surry Court House village — early courts met at various locations
previous_courthouses.fate: Early structures replaced. Records survived transfers.

previous_courthouses.location: Surry Court House — brick courthouse built in the late 18th century
previous_courthouses.fate: Served through the Civil War without destruction. Replaced by the 1923 building.

record_losses.cause: Normal attrition — water damage, vermin, neglect
records_destroyed: some loose papers from the colonial period
record_losses.description: Surry County escaped the worst courthouse fires and Civil War destruction that plagued many Virginia counties. Its south-bank position shielded it from the heaviest fighting of the Peninsula Campaign. Records survive from 1652 — among the earliest continuous county records in Virginia. Losses are attributable to normal attrition rather than catastrophic events.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Surry County's records are a genealogist's treasure — surviving from 1652 with relatively few gaps. If {ancestor_name} lived in Surry County, the chances of finding documentary evidence are better than in most Virginia Tidewater counties.
record_losses.source: Library of Virginia. A Guide to Virginia County and City Records on Microfilm. Richmond, 2001.

deeds.completeness: Very good. Deed books survive from 1652 with relatively few gaps.
deeds.gaps: Some minor gaps in early volumes. Generally continuous.
deeds.repository: Surry County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia, Richmond
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Library of Virginia digital collections; Ancestry.com VA land records

wills_estates.completeness: Very good. Will books and estate inventories from the 1650s onward.
wills_estates.gaps: Some loose estate papers from the earliest period are fragmentary. Bound volumes are intact.
wills_estates.repository: Surry County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Library of Virginia digital collections

marriages.completeness: Marriage bonds from 1768. Earlier marriages documented in court order entries and church records.
marriages.gaps: No formal marriage bonds before 1768. Court order books contain some earlier marriage references.
marriages.repository: Surry County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia

court_minutes.completeness: Very good. Order books from 1652 with few gaps.
court_minutes.gaps: Minor gaps in some volumes. Overall series is among the most complete in Virginia.
court_minutes.repository: Library of Virginia; Surry County Clerk of Court
court_minutes.online_access: Library of Virginia microfilm; published transcriptions for some volumes

vital_records.completeness: Virginia state registration began 1912. Earlier vital events in church records and court orders.
vital_records.repository: Virginia Department of Health, Office of Vital Records

substitute_sources.description: Southwark Parish (Church of England / Episcopal) served Surry County. Vestry minutes and register fragments supplement the county court record.
substitute_sources.repository: Library of Virginia; Virginia Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Parish records provide baptism, burial, and processioning records. Tithable lists in vestry minutes function as population records for the colonial period.
substitute_sources.source: Library of Virginia parish record collections.

substitute_sources.source_type: Published abstracts — Boddie's Surry County histories
substitute_sources.description: John Bennett Boddie published extensive abstracts of Surry County records, including deeds, wills, and court orders from the colonial period.
substitute_sources.repository: Available in major genealogical libraries; reprinted editions
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Boddie's published abstracts are convenient finding aids and provide access to Surry County's colonial records without visiting the courthouse.
substitute_sources.source: Boddie, John Bennett. Colonial Surry. Richmond: Dietz Press, 1948.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790–1940. Surry County's relatively small population makes census research manageable.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Census records complement the excellent county court records. Slave schedules document the large enslaved population.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Surry County, VA.

narrative_hooks: Surry County's records reach back to 1652 — and because the courthouse escaped the fires and wars that destroyed other Virginia courthouses, {ancestor_name}'s deed or will from the colonial period likely still exists in the vault at Surry Court House.

Boddie's published abstracts of Surry County records put the colonial record within reach of anyone with a library card — if {ancestor_name} bought land or proved a will before 1800, the abstract may already be in print.

Surry's south-bank isolation, which made the county feel remote from the north-bank power centers, also protected its courthouse — the war that destroyed records across the river left Surry's paper trail intact.

The same courthouse where Bacon's rebels might have appeared in 1676 was still keeping records when {ancestor_name} proved a will in {year} — three centuries of continuous documentation in one small county.
source_references: Boddie, John Bennett. Colonial Surry. Richmond: Dietz Press, 1948.
Library of Virginia. A Guide to Virginia County and City Records on Microfilm. Richmond, 2001.
Morgan, Edmund S. American Slavery, American Freedom. New York: W. W. Norton, 1975.
National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Surry County, VA.
